title: Livestock heat deaths in transit doubled in UK record-hot summer of 2025 - Carbon Brief
gist: Carbon Brief's FOI analysis found that 6,595 livestock animals — mostly chickens — died from heat stress en route to UK slaughterhouses in summer 2025, double the 3,100 recorded in summer 2024, driven by the UK's record-hot summer that human-caused climate change made 70 times more likely. Cold-stress deaths also nearly doubled over the 2024-25 winter to more than 13,000. Both trends are expected to worsen as climate change intensifies extreme weather frequency.

themes.theme: Heat-stress livestock mortality doubling with climate change

insights.claim: 6,595 livestock animals died from heat stress en route to UK abattoirs in summer 2025 (June–August), double the 3,100 recorded in summer 2024 and far above zero official cases in summer 2023.
insights.speaker: Orla Dwyer
insights.so_what: Quantified, rapidly escalating animal mortality trend signals growing material risk for UK livestock supply chains as temperatures rise.
insights.quote: Twice as many animals died due to heat stress en route to slaughterhouses during the UK's record-hot summer in 2025 compared to 2024, according to new Carbon Brief analysis.

insights.claim: The 6,600 heat-related livestock deaths in summer 2025 remain well below the 18,500+ deaths recorded in summer 2022, when UK temperatures first exceeded 40C, indicating that the peak mortality response to extreme heat events can be far larger than 2025 figures.
insights.speaker: Orla Dwyer
insights.so_what: A 40C-style event in a future summer could produce a mortality spike three times the 2025 level, representing severe supply-chain disruption risk for poultry processors.
insights.quote: These figures were still below the more than 18,500 deaths recorded in the summer of 2022 when UK temperatures hit 40C for the first time.

insights.claim: On a single day — 12 August 2025 — 2,154 chickens died from heat stress, with body temperatures as high as 46C; chickens die when body temperature exceeds 45C.
insights.speaker: Orla Dwyer
insights.so_what: Single-day spikes of this magnitude can overwhelm slaughterhouse logistics and expose poultry companies to regulatory scrutiny and reputational liability.
insights.quote: On 12 August, amid more high temperatures, 2,154 chickens died in heat-stress incidents. Body temperatures of some of the chickens that died on this day were as high as 46C.

insights.claim: Almost 1,000 chickens died in a single heatwave day on 11 July 2025, with vet reports documenting visible heat-stress signs including panting and immobility.
insights.speaker: Orla Dwyer
insights.so_what: Consistent high-mortality days on individual heatwaves demonstrate operational vulnerability that is difficult to mitigate without structural changes to transport logistics.
insights.quote: Almost 1,000 chickens died in a number of incidents during a heatwave on 11 July 2025.

insights.claim: The published 'dead on arrival' data likely underestimates total climate-linked livestock mortality because cases where heat or cold is listed as a contributing factor — but not the primary cause — are excluded from official tallies.
insights.speaker: Orla Dwyer
insights.so_what: Headline mortality figures are a floor, not a ceiling — actual economic losses from climate stress on UK poultry transport are larger than reported.
insights.quote: The figures were likely an underestimate of the total number of livestock deaths due to high or low temperatures, as they only included deaths with 'suspected cold/heat stress' as a listed category.

themes.theme: Climate attribution — record 2025 UK summer heat

insights.claim: The Met Office determined that human-caused climate change made the UK's record-hot summer 2025 70 times more likely to occur than in a pre-industrial climate.
insights.speaker: Orla Dwyer
insights.so_what: A 70x likelihood multiplier from climate change confirms that summer 2025 heat conditions are now a baseline risk rather than a once-in-a-generation anomaly, with direct implications for agricultural and food-sector underwriting.
insights.quote: According to the Met Office, human-caused climate change made this summer heat 70 times more likely to occur.

insights.claim: Summer 2025 was the warmest summer on record in the UK, providing the context for livestock heat-death figures that are twice those of summer 2024.
insights.speaker: Orla Dwyer
insights.so_what: Record-setting UK summer temperatures are now documented as causing measurable doubling of livestock mortality — a direct cost indicator for the food-supply chain.
insights.quote: 6,595 animals died due to heat stress en route to abattoirs between June and August 2025, which was the warmest summer on record in the UK.

insights.claim: More than 13,057 livestock animals died from cold-stress conditions during winter 2024-25 (December–February), more than double the 6,981 deaths in winter 2023-24.
insights.speaker: Orla Dwyer
insights.so_what: Both temperature extremes are simultaneously worsening, creating dual-sided seasonal risk spikes for UK livestock transporters and insurers every year.
insights.quote: At least 13,057 livestock animals died due to cold weather conditions between December 2024 and February 2025. This is more than double the number – 6,981 – that died the previous winter.

insights.claim: On 6 February 2025 alone, 4,056 poultry deaths were reported due to cold-weather impacts, indicating that single-day cold-event mortality can exceed single-day heat-event mortality.
insights.speaker: Orla Dwyer
insights.so_what: Cold-event single-day spikes can be even larger than heat spikes, underscoring that winter supply-chain disruption risk for UK poultry is substantial and growing.
insights.quote: On 6 February 2025 alone, 4,056 poultry deaths were reported due to cold weather impacts.

insights.claim: Cold-stress deaths also occur in summer months, with 326 animal deaths recorded amid cold weather in summer 2023 due to overnight temperature drops — demonstrating year-round, multi-directional climate mortality risk.
insights.speaker: Orla Dwyer
insights.so_what: Risk is not seasonal and unidirectional; overnight cold even in summer creates mortality exposures that transport operators must plan for throughout the year.
insights.quote: Most cold deaths during warmer months occurred in the early hours of the morning or overnight when temperatures dropped.

themes.theme: Regulatory data gaps and government non-response

insights.claim: Scotland and Northern Ireland do not record cause of death for livestock arriving dead at slaughterhouses, making climate-linked mortality statistics incomplete across the UK and suggesting England/Wales figures represent a partial national picture.
insights.speaker: Orla Dwyer
insights.so_what: The true scale of the climate-mortality problem in UK livestock transport is larger than current England/Wales data reveals; investors in UK food companies should assume undercounting.
insights.quote: Scotland and Northern Ireland do not record the cause of deaths en route to slaughterhouses, so it is not possible to single out the cases linked to high or low temperatures.

insights.claim: The FSA acknowledged that 'dead on arrival' incidents caused by cold and heat stress increased by more than 50% between April 2024 and March 2025 versus the prior year, while both the FSA and Defra declined to comment on the new figures.
insights.speaker: Orla Dwyer
insights.so_what: Government acknowledgment of a 50%+ year-on-year rise in temperature-related livestock deaths combined with refusal to comment signals regulatory pressure building without policy response.
insights.quote: The FSA said that the number of 'dead on arrival' incidents caused by cold and heat stress increased by more than 50% between April 2024 and March 2025 compared to the same period the year prior.

insights.claim: Data on the 2025 summer climate deaths was obtained exclusively via UK Freedom of Information Act requests to the FSA, meaning this information is not proactively published and is only accessible through legal disclosure mechanisms.
insights.speaker: Orla Dwyer
insights.so_what: Lack of proactive disclosure by regulators limits market visibility into climate-related food supply chain risks, creating information asymmetries for investors and ESG analysts.
insights.quote: The data was obtained through the UK Freedom of Information (FOI) Act from the Food Standards Agency (FSA), which is responsible for the compliance of slaughterhouses in England and Wales.

themes.theme: Structural vulnerability of poultry vs. other livestock

insights.claim: Chickens represent the vast majority of heat-stress transit deaths; pigs, cows and sheep also die but in smaller numbers, and chickens' inability to sweat makes them disproportionately vulnerable to transport heat stress.
insights.speaker: Orla Dwyer
insights.so_what: UK poultry processors and their logistics partners face higher climate-mortality exposure than red-meat supply chains, making poultry-focused companies more exposed to heat-event losses.
insights.quote: Pigs and chickens cannot sweat and face difficulties cooling down on very hot days.

insights.claim: At least 1 million chickens die annually in UK transport to slaughterhouses due to suffocation, poor procedures and other factors, with climate stress adding an incremental layer of mortality on top of this baseline.
insights.speaker: Orla Dwyer
insights.so_what: Climate-related deaths are a fraction of total UK poultry transport mortality, indicating deep structural inefficiencies in transit logistics that compound climate exposure.
insights.quote: At least 1m chickens die in the UK each year while being transported to slaughterhouses due to suffocation, poor transport procedures and other issues.

insights.claim: Cramped journey conditions and high humidity — sometimes above 80% — exacerbate heat mortality risk for chickens, meaning climate heat combines multiplicatively with operational crowding rather than additively.
insights.speaker: Orla Dwyer
insights.so_what: Mitigating climate heat-mortality requires changes to vehicle density and ventilation design, not just scheduling adjustments — increasing capital expenditure requirements for UK poultry transport operators.
insights.quote: Cramped or long journeys can exacerbate this, combined with high humidity levels, sometimes upwards of 80%, the livestock data showed.

themes.theme: Animal welfare advocacy and systemic food-system critique

insights.claim: Animal Aid campaigns manager Alex Harman argues that climate change is 'amplifying the violence already built into animal farming' and that the 6,600 heat deaths represent prolonged suffering inside metal containers, not merely commodity losses.
insights.speaker: Orla Dwyer
insights.so_what: Rising mortality statistics are being used by animal rights groups to build a systemic critique of intensive livestock farming, increasing reputational and legislative risk for UK meat producers.
insights.quote: These 6,600 individuals [in summer 2025] did not just die, they suffered prolonged, agonising heat exhaustion inside metal containers.

insights.claim: Animal Equality UK executive director Abigail Penny warns that 'these same scenes of extreme animal suffering play out every summer and, if nothing is done, it's only going to get worse', reflecting NGO consensus that current mitigation measures are failing.
insights.speaker: Orla Dwyer
insights.so_what: Sustained NGO pressure combined with worsening mortality data creates a growing probability of UK regulatory tightening on livestock transport standards, raising compliance costs for the sector.
insights.quote: "these same scenes of extreme animal suffering play out every summer and, if nothing is done, it's only going to get worse"

themes.theme: Existing mitigation measures and their limits

insights.claim: UK government guidance already requires livestock transport workers to ensure vehicle water and ventilation systems function properly, avoid travel during peak heat or cold, and recognise stress signs — but these measures have not prevented mortality from doubling year-on-year.
insights.speaker: Orla Dwyer
insights.so_what: Existing soft-guidance mitigation has proven inadequate as climate extremes intensify; harder regulatory mandates or capital investment in refrigerated transport are likely policy responses.
insights.quote: Workers transporting animals during extreme weather conditions are expected to put in place measures to protect them, according to UK government guidance.
